description: Tokyo is a city of extraordinary contrasts — ultramodern skyscrapers beside ancient temples, Michelin-starred restaurants steps from tiny ramen stalls, silent shrines amid neon-drenched entertainment districts. As one of the world's largest metropolitan areas, Tokyo delivers an almost overwhelming density of experience: world-class museums, obsessive shopping culture, impeccably safe streets, and a transit network so precise it redefines punctuality. Whether you're chasing street fashion in Harajuku, slurping tonkotsu at midnight in Shinjuku, or meditating in a moss-covered garden in Yanaka, Tokyo rewards curiosity at every turn.

neighborhoodRecs: Shinjuku — entertainment, nightlife, and shopping epicenter with both Kabukicho's neon chaos and the tranquil Golden Gai bar alleys
Shibuya — iconic scramble crossing, youth fashion, Daikanyama's boutiques, and Nakameguro's canal-side cafes
Yanaka — preserved Edo-era shitamachi neighborhood with temples, traditional shops, and a genuine local atmosphere
Asakusa — historic district anchored by Senso-ji Temple, rickshaws, craft shops, and excellent budget eating
Shimokitazawa — bohemian enclave of vintage clothing stores, indie live music venues, and artisan coffee
Akihabara — electronics and anime culture mecca, perfect for tech enthusiasts and pop culture fans
areasToAvoid: Kabukicho's back streets late at night — host bar touts aggressively target tourists and bills can be fraudulently inflated
Poorly lit side streets in Roppongi after 2am — drug solicitation and aggressive bar entry tactics persist
Unofficial currency exchange booths near Ueno — rates are significantly worse than bank ATMs
scamWarnings: Roppongi bar overcharge scam: friendly strangers invite you to a bar, you receive an enormous bill; always check prices before ordering
Taxi drivers taking scenic routes from Narita — always confirm fixed-price options or use the Narita Express train
Fake monks in tourist areas collecting 'donations' — legitimate Buddhist monks do not solicit money on streets
Pachinko parlor pressure: staff may discourage leaving after converting winnings; you have every right to leave
Unofficial rickshaw operators near Asakusa quoting prices in 'per person' but billing per ride segment

airportTransfer.cheapest: Narita Express (N'EX) to Shinjuku: ¥3,070 (~$21 USD), ~90 minutes. Limousine Bus to major hotels: ¥3,200, ~90-120 min
airportTransfer.fastest: Narita Express to Tokyo Station: 53 minutes, ¥3,070. From Haneda: Keikyu Line or Tokyo Monorail, 30-40 min, ~¥600
airportTransfer.recommended: Narita Express (N'EX) IC Card for convenience — purchase the N'EX + Suica combo pass at the airport for ¥4,000 (includes ¥1,500 Suica balance)

tippingEtiquette: Tipping is not practiced in Japan and can occasionally cause offense — excellent service is considered standard
Never tip at restaurants, taxis, hotels, or hair salons
If you want to express gratitude, a small gift (omiyage) is far more culturally appropriate than cash
Some high-end ryokan include a service charge; this is communal and no additional tip is expected

dailyCostBreakdown.accommodation: Budget: ¥3,000-5,000 capsule/hostel. Mid: ¥12,000-20,000 business hotel. Premium: ¥35,000+ boutique/luxury
dailyCostBreakdown.meals: Budget: ¥800-1,500 per meal (convenience store, ramen, yoshoku). Mid: ¥1,500-4,000. Premium: ¥8,000+ omakase
dailyCostBreakdown.transport: ¥1,000-1,500/day IC card (Suica/Pasmo) covers all metro and JR lines within Tokyo
dailyCostBreakdown.activities: Most temples and shrines free. Museums: ¥500-2,000. TeamLab: ¥3,200. Day trip to Nikko: ¥5,000+

packingTips: Bring a small daypack — you will walk enormous distances and accumulate purchases
Pocket Wi-Fi or SIM card is essential; buy at the airport for connectivity on the extensive subway network
Comfortable walking shoes are non-negotiable — 20,000+ steps per day is common
Carry cash: many small restaurants, vending machines, and shrines are cash-only
Pack light layers for spring and fall; summers demand lightweight, breathable fabrics
A small umbrella or packable rain jacket handles sudden showers, especially in June and September
Bring or buy a Suica IC card — it works on virtually all trains, buses, and convenience store purchases
premium.bookingStrategy: Book accommodation 3-6 months ahead for cherry blossom season (late March-early April) and Golden Week (late April-early May) — the city fills completely. Narita Express passes and rail passes can be purchased abroad more cheaply. For omakase restaurants, reservations open exactly one month in advance and fill within hours; use platforms like Tableall or Omakase.app. Avoid the 'Japan Rail Pass' if staying only in Tokyo — a Suica card is more economical for local travel.
localTips: Get a Suica card at any JR station — it works on subways, JR trains, buses, and even at 7-Eleven
Convenience stores (7-Eleven, FamilyMart, Lawson) serve genuinely excellent food at low prices — don't skip them
Google Maps works perfectly for Tokyo transit navigation, including platform numbers
Speak quietly on trains and never take phone calls — it's considered very rude
Free public Wi-Fi exists at major stations and 7-Eleven stores; pocket Wi-Fi is more reliable
Department store basement food halls (depachika) offer extraordinary quality prepared foods and sweets
Many restaurants display plastic food models or have picture menus — pointing is perfectly acceptable
Coin lockers at major stations let you drop luggage and explore hands-free
Shibuya Sky, Tokyo Skytree, and Tokyo Tower all offer spectacular city views — book Shibuya Sky online to skip queues
premium.bestTimeToVisit: Late March to early April for cherry blossoms (book 6 months ahead). October-November for autumn foliage and comfortable temperatures. December and January are quiet, clear, and cold — excellent for budget travelers. Avoid Golden Week (late April-early May) and Obon (mid-August) for domestic tourist crowds.
dayTrips: Nikko — UNESCO shrines and stunning mountain scenery, 2 hours by Tobu or JR limited express
Kamakura — Great Buddha, coastal temples, and hiking trails, 1 hour from Shinjuku via Shonan-Shinjuku Line
Hakone — Mt. Fuji views, hot springs (onsen), and open-air sculpture museum, 85 min by Romancecar
Yokohama — Chinatown, harbor, Sankeien Garden, and Ramen Museum, 30 min from Shibuya
Kawaguchiko — best base for Mt. Fuji views and lake activities, 2 hours by Fujikyuko Line
Chichibu — soba, cherry blossoms, scenic railway, and night festival, 90 min via Seibu Ikebukuro Line
visaInfo: Citizens of the US, UK, EU countries, Canada, and Australia: visa-free for up to 90 days for tourism — no advance application needed; entry granted on arrival at Japanese immigration
Japan requires a return or onward ticket and proof of sufficient funds — have your return booking and some cash or bank statement ready for immigration in case you are asked
Visit Japan Web: pre-register your passport and customs declaration online for faster processing — recommended for busy arrival periods at Narita or Haneda
Narita Airport (NRT) and Haneda Airport (HND) are both major international gateways — Haneda is closer to central Tokyo

Japan has strict drug laws — many common medications (pseudoephedrine cold medicines, certain ADHD medications, some sleep aids) are illegal even with a foreign prescription; check Japanese Customs website before packing
currencyTips: Currency: Japanese Yen (¥ / JPY). Despite being a highly advanced economy, Japan remains heavily cash-based — carry far more cash than you would in most developed countries
7-Eleven ATMs (found in all 7-Eleven convenience stores) and Japan Post ATMs accept most foreign Visa/Mastercard reliably — the most consistent option for foreign card withdrawals
Cards are accepted at department stores (Isetan, Takashimaya), chains, and larger restaurants but many ramen shops, izakaya, and temple stalls are cash only
IC cards (Suica, Pasmo): available at all train stations; load with cash and use on any train, bus, and at convenience stores — essential for navigating Tokyo
Exchange at the post office (Japan Post) often offers competitive rates; hotels offer convenience but not the best exchange
premium.simCard: IIJmio and b-mobile sell popular tourist SIMs at Narita (NRT) and Haneda (HND) airports. A 30-day data SIM with 20–50 GB costs ¥3,000–7,000. Data-only SIMs are the standard tourist option (voice plans require a local address). Pocket WiFi devices (rent at the airport) are popular for groups. eSIM via Airalo is widely available for Japan and activates before you land.
